sentence: Identify non-fluoropolymer thermoplastic material for heat shrink tubing that could replace FEP heat shrink tubing with an application/service temperature of up to 180 °C and a shrink temperature under 250 °C. Additionally, evaluate whether specific material formulations or processing approaches could help meet these performance requirements.

required: Barrier layer must not impede shrink

extracted_keywords: Barrier layer must not impede shrink
extracted_constraints: No PFAS – The finished tubing must contain no detectable PFAS or PFAS‑derived substances (≤ 0.1 µg kg⁻¹, as defined by EU PFAS‑restriction guidance).

BPA‑free – The material must be free of bisphenol‑A; BPA migration into a food simulant (3 % acetic acid at 40 °C, 10 days) must be < 0.2 mg L⁻¹ (EU SML) or below the FDA 21 CFR 177 specific migration limit.

Food‑contact compliance – Must meet FDA 21 CFR 177 and EU Regulation (EU) No 10/2011 requirements; all leachables must stay below the regulatory specific migration limits for the corresponding food simulants.

Medical‑device biocompatibility – If intended for medical use, the tubing must satisfy ISO 10993‑3 cytotoxicity, ISO 10993‑4 skin‑irritation, and ISO 10993‑5 systemic toxicity requirements (no adverse biological response at the tested concentrations).
Continuous service temperature – The material must retain its dimensional and mechanical integrity at a continuous service temperature of at least 180 °C.
Heat‑shrink activation – The tubing must reach full shrink at a temperature ≤ 250 °C (activation temperature ≤ 250 °C).
Shrink ratio – Upon activation the tube must shrink to ≤ 10 % of its original diameter (≥ 90 % shrink ratio).
Tensile strength after shrink – Post‑shrink the tube must exhibit tensile strength ≥ 60 MPa (ASTM D 5386).
Tear resistance after shrink – Post‑shrink the tube must sustain a tear load ≥ 5000 N/m (ASTM D 4581).
Compression set – After 100 shrink cycles at 180 °C, the tube’s compression set must be ≤ 10 % of its original wall thickness.
Barrier properties – Oxygen permeability must be ≤ 1 × 10⁻⁷ cm³·mm m⁻² s⁻¹ and water‑vapor transmission ≤ 1 × 10⁻⁹ g mm m⁻² day, meeting or exceeding FEP performance.
Chemical resistance – The tubing must resist degradation in 5 % HCl, 5 % NaOH, and common organic solvents (e.g., acetone, toluene) at 180 °C without loss of mechanical properties.
Hydrocarbon stability – The material must not become brittle or degrade when exposed to gasoline or diesel at 100–150 °C.

Sterilization tolerance – The tubing must survive steam sterilization at 121 °C for 15 min, ethylene oxide at 55 °C, or γ‑irradiation up to 25 kGy without appreciable loss of tensile strength, tear resistance, or barrier performance.
Residual stress control – Post‑extrusion and heat treatment, the tubing must not exhibit residual stresses that cause warping or dimensional distortion when cooled to ambient temperature.
